generated_oml:
// C3: Physical Sensing Components
instance InternalTemperatureSensor1 : DTDFVocab:SensingComponent [
    base:desc "High-precision DS18S20 1-Wire digital thermometer measuring internal temperature of the incubator box with ±0.5°C accuracy (-55°C to +125°C range). Positioned uniformly distributed inside the styrofoam container."
    DTDFVocab:producedData InternalTemperatureDataStream1
]

instance InternalTemperatureSensor2 : DTDFVocab:SensingComponent [
    base:desc "High-precision DS18S20 1-Wire digital thermometer measuring internal temperature of the incubator box with ±0.5°C accuracy (-55°C to +125°C range). Positioned uniformly distributed inside the styrofoam container."
    DTDFVocab:producedData InternalTemperatureDataStream2
]

instance ExternalTemperatureSensor : DTDFVocab:SensingComponent [
    base:desc "High-precision DS18S20 1-Wire digital thermometer measuring ambient external temperature with ±0.5°C accuracy (-55°C to +125°C range). Positioned outside the styrofoam container."
    DTDFVocab:producedData ExternalTemperatureDataStream
]

// C4: Physical-to-Virtual Interaction
instance InternalTemperatureDataStream1 : DTDFVocab:DataTransmitted [
    base:desc "Periodic temperature data stream from InternalTemperatureSensor1 transmitted via 1-Wire bus to RabbitMQ message broker for DT processing."
    DTDFVocab:producedFrom InternalTemperatureSensor1
]

instance InternalTemperatureDataStream2 : DTDFVocab:DataTransmitted [
    base:desc "Periodic temperature data stream from InternalTemperatureSensor2 transmitted via 1-Wire bus to RabbitMQ message broker for DT processing."
    DTDFVocab:producedFrom InternalTemperatureSensor2
]

instance ExternalTemperatureDataStream : DTDFVocab:DataTransmitted [
    base:desc "Periodic ambient temperature data stream from ExternalTemperatureSensor transmitted via 1-Wire bus to RabbitMQ message broker for DT processing."
    DTDFVocab:producedFrom ExternalTemperatureSensor
]

// C10: Models/Data
instance TwoParameterThermalModel : DTDFVocab:Model [
    base:desc "Physics-based differential equation model with 2 free parameters representing simplified thermal dynamics of the incubator system."
    DTDFVocab:inputTo PythonCalibrationEnabler, DifferentialEquationSolverEnabler
]

instance FourParameterThermalModel : DTDFVocab:Model [
    base:desc "Physics-based differential equation model with 4 free parameters representing detailed thermal dynamics including heatbed thermal inertia."
    DTDFVocab:inputTo PythonCalibrationEnabler, DifferentialEquationSolverEnabler
]

instance RealTimeTemperatureData : DTDFVocab:Data [
    base:desc "Aggregated real-time temperature data from all sensors (2 internal, 1 external) used for model calibration and monitoring."
    DTDFVocab:fromData InternalTemperatureDataStream1, InternalTemperatureDataStream2, ExternalTemperatureDataStream
    DTDFVocab:inputTo PythonCalibrationEnabler, KalmanFilterEnabler
]

instance ControlCommandData : DTDFVocab:Data [
    base:desc "Actuation commands and system responses from Raspberry Pi controller used for closed-loop control optimization."
    DTDFVocab:inputTo ControllerOptimizationEnabler
]

instance HistoricalTemperatureData : DTDFVocab:Data [
    base:desc "Historical temperature data used for co-simulation and long-term trend analysis."
    DTDFVocab:inputTo DifferentialEquationSolverEnabler
]

// C11: Enablers
instance PythonCalibrationEnabler : DTDFVocab:Enabler [
    base:desc "Python-based implementation of non-linear least squares solvers for model parameter estimation using Scipy optimization library."
    DTDFVocab:enables ModelCalibrationService, AnomalyDetectionService
]

instance DifferentialEquationSolverEnabler : DTDFVocab:Enabler [
    base:desc "Numerical solver for differential equation models implementing thermal dynamics simulation."
    DTDFVocab:enables SimulationService, WhatIfSimulationService
]

instance RabbitMQMessageBrokerEnabler : DTDFVocab:Enabler [
    base:desc "Message broker facilitating cyber-physical communication between Raspberry Pi controller and DT components."
    DTDFVocab:enables RealTimeMonitoringService
]

instance KalmanFilterEnabler : DTDFVocab:Enabler [
    base:desc "Kalman filter implementation for state estimation and anomaly detection in temperature measurements."
    DTDFVocab:enables AnomalyDetectionService, ModelCalibrationService
]

instance ControllerOptimizationEnabler : DTDFVocab:Enabler [
    base:desc "Optimization algorithms for closed-loop controller reconfiguration based on real-time performance data."
    DTDFVocab:enables ControllerOptimizationService
]

// C6: Services
instance RealTimeMonitoringService : DTDFVocab:Service [
    base:desc "Continuous collection and processing of sensor data streams from the physical twin for state estimation and performance tracking."
    DTDFVocab:provides RealTimeTemperatureInsight
]

instance ModelCalibrationService : DTDFVocab:Service [
    base:desc "Automated calibration of thermal models using real-time sensor data to maintain high-fidelity representation of physical twin behavior."
    DTDFVocab:provides ModelAccuracyInsight, CalibrationAction
]

instance SimulationService : DTDFVocab:Service [
    base:desc "Execution of dynamic simulations using calibrated thermal models to predict current thermal behavior under operating conditions."
    DTDFVocab:provides ThermalPredictionInsight
]

instance WhatIfSimulationService : DTDFVocab:Service [
    base:desc "Co-simulation with historical and real-time data to evaluate hypothetical operating scenarios and their potential impacts."
    DTDFVocab:provides ScenarioAnalysisInsight
]

instance AnomalyDetectionService : DTDFVocab:Service [
    base:desc "Monitoring algorithms detecting deviations from safe/optimal operating conditions using Kalman filter-based state estimation."
    DTDFVocab:provides AnomalyDetectionInsight, CorrectiveAction
]

instance ControllerOptimizationService : DTDFVocab:Service [
    base:desc "Closed-loop reconfiguration of control logic based on real-time performance data and model predictions."
    DTDFVocab:provides OptimizationInsight, ControlAdjustmentAction
]

// C17: Insights/Actions
instance RealTimeTemperatureInsight : DTDFVocab:Insight [
    base:desc "Continuous visualization and analysis of current internal and external temperature measurements for operational monitoring."
]

]

    base:desc "Predicted thermal behavior under current operating conditions based on calibrated differential equation models."
]

]

]

]

    base:desc "Automated adjustment of model parameters using non-linear least squares optimization to minimize prediction errors."
    DTDFVocab:IsAutomatic true
]

    DTDFVocab:IsAutomatic true
]

instance ControlAdjustmentAction : DTDFVocab:Action [
    base:desc "Dynamic reconfiguration of heatbed power and fan speed based on real-time performance data and model predictions."

]

instance HeatbedActuator : DTDFVocab:ActingComponent [
    base:desc "Resistive heating element responsible for raising internal temperature of the incubator box with controlled power limits to prevent overheating."
]

]

    base:desc "Embedded system implementing bang-bang control strategy for temperature regulation through bidirectional communication with DT via RabbitMQ."
]

instance virtual_to_physical_interaction : DTDFVocab:VirtualToPhysical [
    base:desc "The **virtual-to-physical interaction** in the incubator DT system is mediated by the **controller** (implemented on a Raspberry Pi) and the **RabbitMQ communication server**. The controller receives **temperature measurements** from the physical twin (PT) and issues **control commands** (e.g., activation/deactivation of the **heatbed** and **fan**) via the RabbitMQ server. The **command types** are not explicitly detailed, but the controller employs a **bang-bang-like control strategy** with parameters: **Lower Limit (LL)**, **Upper Limit (UL)**, **Heating Duration (H)**, and **Waiting Duration (C)**. No **validation/authentication mechanisms**, **feedback systems**, or **conflict resolution** protocols are described. The **command execution confirmation** is not explicitly mentioned, though the controller statechart (Figure 8) implies a **closed-loop feedback mechanism** where the PT’s response (temperature changes) is monitored. The DT does not directly send commands to the PT; instead, the **controller acts as an intermediary**, receiving inputs from the DT (via RabbitMQ) and translating them into physical actions."
]

instance twinning_time_scale : DTDFVocab:TimeScale [
    base:desc "The Digital Twin system for the incubator case study operates with temporal synchronization driven by periodic sensor data acquisition and controller actions. The physical twin (PT) is monitored via **two internal temperature sensors** and **one external temperature sensor**, with data collected at unspecified but implied high-frequency intervals (likely millisecond-to-second range) to ensure real-time responsiveness. The cyber-physical system (CPS) employs a **Raspberry Pi-based controller** that regulates heating via a **heatbed** and a **fan**, with control parameters including **heating duration** and **waiting duration** (exact values not specified). The Digital Twin integrates these data streams to enable **real-time visualization** of the incubator's status, requiring low-latency data relay to a **time-series database** and subsequent dashboard rendering. Calibration processes (e.g., for Model B) are triggered **on-demand** to adjust model parameters dynamically, ensuring alignment between the PT and DT. The system leverages **co-simulation** (implied via historical/real-time data relay) for predictive analysis, though exact synchronization frequencies or latency constraints are not detailed in the provided documents. The temporal granularity is dictated by the sensor sampling rate and controller execution cycle, which must be sufficiently fine to capture the **thermal inertia** of the heatbed and the **dynamic response** of the insulated container. No explicit mention of hard real-time guarantees or deterministic latency requirements is provided."
]

instance dt_constellation : DTDFVocab:Constellation [
    base:desc "The Digital Twin system for the incubator follows a **CPS-based architecture**, integrating **cyber** (controller, communication, and simulation models) and **physical** (sensors, actuators, and the insulated container) components. The orchestration is structured around three interdependent layers:
  1. **Data Layer**: Sensors (two internal, one external temperature sensors) feed real-time data into the system, which is then stored in a **time-series database** for visualization and analysis.
  2. **Model Layer**: Two simulation models (Model A and Model B) are employed, with Model B preferred for its accuracy in predicting **heatbed thermal inertia**. These models are **calibrated dynamically** using data from the PT to ensure fidelity.
  3. **Algorithm Layer**: Includes **calibration algorithms** (e.g., for adjusting model parameters), **monitoring algorithms** (e.g., anomaly detection via unspecified techniques), and **optimization algorithms** (e.g., controller parameter tuning for what-if simulations).
  The system employs a **closed-loop control flow**: calibration algorithms update models based on PT data, monitors (e.g., anomaly detectors) assess system performance, and humans or automated processes intervene to reconfigure the PT or DT as needed. The architecture is **monolithic** in scope, with no explicit mention of modular or microservice-based decomposition. Integration between layers is achieved via **RabbitMQ** (implied for communication) and a **Raspberry Pi controller**, which orchestrates the interaction between the cyber and physical components."
]

instance life_cycle_stages : DTDFVocab:EvolutionStage [
    base:desc "The Digital Twin lifecycle for the incubator case study includes the following phases: **(1) Conceptualization and Hardware Specification**: Defined by the physical twin (PT) comprising an insulated styrofoam box, heatbed, fan, and three temperature sensors (two internal, one external) controlled via a Raspberry Pi. **(2) Model Development**: Two physics-based simulation models were constructed—one with **two free parameters** and another with **four free parameters**—to predict internal temperature dynamics. **(3) Calibration and Validation**: Models were calibrated using a **non-linear least squares solver** (implied by the calibration method in Section 3) and experimentally validated against real-world data (Section 4). **(4) Deployment and Monitoring**: The Digital Twin integrates real-time sensor data from the PT to inform monitors checking for safe and optimal performance (as described in the general DT framework in Document 1). **(5) Evolution and Adaptation**: Implicitly supported by the calibration process, which updates models to minimize residual error between predicted and observed behavior, enabling adaptive refinement. **Governance** is not explicitly detailed but inferred from the structured repository ([2]) hosting hardware/software specifications, suggesting version control and reproducibility practices. The lifecycle ends with experimental validation (Section 4), with future work (Section 5) hinting at iterative refinement for robustness and predictability."
]

instance fidelity_and_validity_considerations : DTDFVocab:FidelityConsideration [
    base:desc "
  **Model Fidelity and Validity:**
  - **Model Fidelity Requirements:**
    - **Accuracy vs. Complexity Trade-off:** The 4-parameter model outperformed the 2-parameter model in predicting thermal inertia, demonstrating higher fidelity at the cost of computational complexity.
    - **Abstraction Levels:** Models may exist at multiple levels (e.g., simplified for quick simulations, detailed for precise predictions).
    - **Environmental Context:** Models must account for external factors (e.g., ambient temperature) to maintain validity.

  - **Validation and Verification Methods:**
    - **Experimental Validation:** Real-world experiments (Document 2, Section 4) compared model predictions to actual PT behavior (e.g., temperature trajectories).
    - **Calibration:** Iterative parameter tuning using sensor data to minimize prediction errors (e.g., thermal inertia adjustment).
    - **Cross-Validation:** Implied by comparing two models; the better-performing model was selected for further DT development.

  - **Uncertainty Sources and Quantification:**
    - **Sensor Noise:** Potential inaccuracies in temperature measurements (not quantified).
    - **Model Assumptions:** Simplifications (e.g., ignoring air convection details) may introduce errors.
    - **Environmental Variability:** Unpredictable factors (e.g., door openings, content changes) affect PT behavior.
    - **Quantification:** Not explicitly detailed, but the model comparison suggests quantitative metrics (e.g., RMSE, R²) were used.

  - **Error Detection and Correction Mechanisms:**
    - **Real-Time Monitoring:** Monitors in the DT detect deviations (e.g., temperature drift) and trigger corrective actions (e.g., recalibration or controller adjustments).
    - **Feedback Loops:** Calibration algorithms adjust model parameters dynamically to reduce errors.
    - **Human-in-the-Loop:** Engineers may intervene if automated corrections fail (e.g., manual parameter tuning).

  - **Quality Assurance Processes:**
    - **Open-Source Transparency:** The incubator’s hardware/software is publicly available, enabling community validation and improvement.
    - **Reproducibility:** Standardized experiments (Document 2) ensure results can be replicated.
    - **Continuous Improvement:** Future work (Document 3, Section 5) hints at iterative refinement of models/algorithms.
    - **Safety Checks:** Monitors ensure the PT operates within safe bounds (e.g., no overheating).
  "
]

instance dt_technical_connection : DTDFVocab:TechnicalConnection [
    base:desc "The **technical network infrastructure** for the incubator DT relies on a **RabbitMQ message broker** deployed on the **Raspberry Pi** (hosting both the PT controller and the communication server). The network architecture is **centralized**, with the RabbitMQ server acting as the sole intermediary for **cyber-physical data exchange**. No details on **communication protocols** (e.g., AMQP version), **network topology** (e.g., star/bus), **bandwidth/latency requirements**, or **security protocols** (e.g., TLS/SSL) are provided. The system assumes **low-latency, high-reliability communication** due to its **on-premise, single-node deployment**, but no redundancy mechanisms (e.g., failover) or **quantitative specifications** (e.g., throughput) are documented. The infrastructure is **tightly coupled** with the PT’s hardware (Raspberry Pi) and lacks explicit **scalability or fault-tolerance features** beyond the basic RabbitMQ reliability."
]

instance dt_hosting_deployment : DTDFVocab:Deployment [
    base:desc "The **hosting and deployment infrastructure** for the incubator DT is **on-premise**, with all critical components (RabbitMQ server, low-level driver, and controller) running on a **single Raspberry Pi**. The system does not require **cloud/edge hybrid models** or distributed architectures. **Computational requirements** are minimal, as the DT leverages the **Raspberry Pi’s embedded resources** without explicit hardware specifications (e.g., RAM/cores). No **scalability mechanisms** (e.g., containerization via Docker/Kubernetes) or **storage requirements** are detailed. **Availability/reliability** is ensured by the **localized deployment**, but no redundancy (e.g., backup nodes) or **high-availability protocols** are described. The DT’s **software stack** is lightweight, relying solely on the **RabbitMQ broker** and **custom control logic** without external dependencies."
]

instance security_and_safety_considerations : DTDFVocab:SecuritySafety [
    base:desc "The Digital Twin (DT) system for the incubator case study incorporates **safety-critical considerations** through its integration of **monitoring and calibration algorithms** that ensure the system operates within predefined operational boundaries. Specifically, the DT employs **monitors** (as depicted in Figure 1) to verify whether the **Cyber-Physical System (CPS)** and its environment are performing **safely and optimally** under real-time conditions. These monitors likely rely on **real-time sensor data** (e.g., temperature sensors inside and outside the insulated container) to detect deviations or anomalies. The system also includes **calibration algorithms** to update the **thermal models** of the CPS (e.g., the heatbed and styrofoam box) with the best estimate of parameters, ensuring accurate predictions of the physical twin's behavior. While the documents do not explicitly mention **cybersecurity protocols** (e.g., encryption, access control, or authentication systems), the **Raspberry Pi-based controller** managing the incubator implies a need for **secure communication** between the cyber (software) and physical (hardware) components. The **fail-safe mechanisms** are indirectly referenced through the **monitoring and calibration loops**, which likely trigger corrective actions (e.g., adjusting the heatbed or fan) if unsafe conditions (e.g., overheating) are detected. However, no explicit **risk assessment approaches** (e.g., ISO 26262, IEC 61508) or **data protection regulations** (e.g., GDPR, HIPAA) are mentioned in the provided context."
]
